questions.question: In Python, what does a variable actually hold?
choices: A reference (pointer) to an object in memory
The memory address as an integer
questions.explanation: Python variables are references to objects. When you assign a = 3, the variable 'a' holds a reference to the integer object 3 in memory, not the value itself.

questions.question: The ___ operator tests whether two variables refer to the exact same object in memory, rather than just equal values.
questions.explanation: The 'is' operator checks object identity (same memory location), while == checks value equality. For example, a is b is True only if a and b point to the same object.

questions.question: What happens when no variables reference a Python object?
choices: It is reclaimed by garbage collection
questions.explanation: Python uses garbage collection (primarily reference counting) to reclaim memory. When an object's reference count drops to zero, its memory is automatically freed.

questions.question: Given a = [1, 2, 3] and b = a, what happens when you run b.append(4)?
choices: Only b becomes [1, 2, 3, 4]; a is unchanged
Both a and b become [1, 2, 3, 4] because they reference the same list
questions.explanation: Since b = a creates a shared reference (both point to the same list object), mutating the list through b also affects a. This is a key consequence of Python's reference-based variable model.

questions.question: The built-in function ___ returns the type of an object.
questions.explanation: type(obj) returns the type (class) of an object. For example, type(42) returns <class 'int'> and type('hello') returns <class 'str'>.

questions.question: In Python, types are associated with:
questions.explanation: In Python's dynamic typing model, types live with objects, not variables. A variable is just a name that can reference any type of object at any time. This is why x = 42 followed by x = 'hello' is perfectly valid.

questions.question: Given a = [1, 2, 3], which of the following creates a true copy rather than a shared reference?
choices: b = a * 1 and b = a are the same
It is impossible to copy a list
questions.explanation: a[:] creates a shallow copy of the list — a new list object with the same elements. After b = a[:], modifying b does not affect a. In contrast, b = a creates a shared reference.
